title: The Northern Cobbler
content:
I.
WAïŋ―IT till our Sally cooms in, fur thou mun aïŋ― sights1 to tell.
Eh, but I be maïŋ―in glad to seeïŋ― tha sa ïŋ―arty anïŋ― well.
ïŋ―Cast awaïŋ―y on a disolut land wiïŋ― a vartical soon2!ïŋ―
Strange fur to goïŋ― fur to think what saïŋ―ilors aïŋ― seeïŋ―n anïŋ― aïŋ― doon;
ïŋ―Summat to drinkïŋ―saïŋ― ïŋ―ot?ïŋ― I ïŋ―a nowt but Adamïŋ―s wine:
Whatïŋ―s the ïŋ―eïŋ―t oïŋ― this little ïŋ―ill-side to the ïŋ―eïŋ―t oïŋ― the line?

II.
ïŋ―Whatïŋ―s iïŋ― tha bottle a-stanning theer? Iïŋ―ll tell tha. Gin.
But if thou wants thy grog, tha mun goïŋ― fur it down to the inn.
Naayïŋ―fur I be maïŋ―in-glad, but thaw tha was iver sa dry,
Thou gits naw gin froïŋ― the bottle theer, anïŋ― Iïŋ―ll tell tha why.

III.
Meïŋ― anïŋ― thy sister was married, when wur it? back-end oïŋ― June,
Ten year sinïŋ―, and wa ïŋ―greed as well as a fiddle iïŋ― tune:
I could fettle and clump owd booïŋ―ts and shoes wiïŋ― the best on ïŋ―em all,
As fer as froïŋ― Thursby thurn hup to Harmsby and Hutterby Hall.
We was busy as beeïŋ―s iïŋ― the bloom anïŋ― ïŋ―appy as ïŋ―art could think,
Anïŋ― then the babby wur burn, and then I taïŋ―kes to the drink.

IV.
Anïŋ― I weant gaïŋ―insaïŋ―y it, my lad, thaw I be hafe shaïŋ―med on it now,
We could sing a good song at the Plow, we could sing a good song at the Plow;
Thaw once of a frosty night I slitherïŋ―d an hurted my huck,3
Anïŋ― I coomïŋ―d neck-an-crop soomtimes slaïŋ―pe down iïŋ― the squad anïŋ― the muck:
Anïŋ― once I fowt wiïŋ― the Taïŋ―ilorïŋ―not hafe ov a man, my ladïŋ―
Fur he scrawmïŋ―d anïŋ― scratted my faïŋ―ce like a cat, anïŋ― it maïŋ―deïŋ―er sa mad
That Sally she turnïŋ―d a tongue-banger4 anïŋ― raïŋ―ted ma, ïŋ―Sottinïŋ― thy braïŋ―ins
Guzzlinïŋ― anïŋ― soïŋ―kinïŋ― anïŋ― smoïŋ―kinïŋ― anïŋ― hawminïŋ―5 about iïŋ― the laïŋ―nes,
Soïŋ― sow-droonk that tha doesn not touch thy ïŋ―at to the Squire;ïŋ―
Anïŋ― I looïŋ―kïŋ―d cock-eyed at my noïŋ―se anïŋ― I seeïŋ―d ïŋ―im a-gittinïŋ― oïŋ― fire;
But sinïŋ― I wur hallus iïŋ― liquor anïŋ― hallus as droonk as a king,
Foïŋ―lksïŋ― coostom flitted awaïŋ―y like a kite wiïŋ― a brokken string.

V.
Anïŋ― Sally she weshïŋ―d foïŋ―lksïŋ― cloïŋ―ths to keep the wolf froïŋ― the door,
Eh but the moor she riled me, she druv me to drink the moor,
Fur I funïŋ―, when ïŋ―er hack wur turnïŋ―d, wheer Sallyïŋ―s owd stockinïŋ― wur ïŋ―id,
Anïŋ― I grabbïŋ―d the munny she maïŋ―de, and I weïŋ―rïŋ―d it oïŋ― liquor, I did.

VI.
Anïŋ― one night I cooms ïŋ―oïŋ―m like a bull gotten loose at a faïŋ―ir,
Anïŋ― she wur a-waïŋ―itinïŋ― foïŋ―mma, anïŋ― cryinïŋ― and teïŋ―rinïŋ― ïŋ―er ïŋ―aïŋ―ir,
Anïŋ― I tummled athurt the craïŋ―dle anïŋ― sweïŋ―rïŋ―d as Iïŋ―d break ivry stick
Oïŋ― furnitur ïŋ―ere iïŋ― the ïŋ―ouse, anïŋ― I gied our Sally a kick,
Anïŋ― I mashïŋ―d the taïŋ―bles anïŋ― chairs, anïŋ― she anïŋ― the babby beïŋ―lïŋ―d,6
Fur I knawïŋ―d naw moor what I did nor a mortal beïŋ―st oïŋ― the feïŋ―ld.

VII.
Anïŋ― when I waïŋ―ked iïŋ― the murninïŋ― I seeïŋ―d that our Sally went laïŋ―med
Cosïŋ― oïŋ― the kick as I gied ïŋ―er, anïŋ― I wur dreadful ashaïŋ―med;
Anïŋ― Sally wur sloomy7 anïŋ― draggle taïŋ―ilïŋ―d in an owd turn gown,
Anïŋ― the babbyïŋ―s faïŋ―ce wurnïŋ―t weshïŋ―d anïŋ― the ïŋ―ole ïŋ―ouse hupside down.

VIII.
Anïŋ― then I minded our Sally sa patty anïŋ― neïŋ―t anïŋ― sweeïŋ―t,
Strait as a pole anïŋ― cleïŋ―n as a flower froïŋ― ïŋ―ead to feeïŋ―t:
Anïŋ― then I minded the fust kiss I gied ïŋ―er by Thursby thurn;
Theer wur a lark a-singinïŋ― ïŋ―is best of a Sunday at murn,
Couldnïŋ―t see ïŋ―im, we ïŋ―eïŋ―rd ïŋ―im a-mountinïŋ― oop ïŋ―igher anïŋ― ïŋ―igher,
Anïŋ― then ïŋ―e turnïŋ―d to the sun, anïŋ― ïŋ―e shined like a sparkle oïŋ― fire.
ïŋ―Doesnïŋ―t tha see ïŋ―im,ïŋ― she axes, ïŋ―fur I can see ïŋ―im?ïŋ― anïŋ― I
Seeïŋ―d nobbut the smile oïŋ― the sun as danced in ïŋ―er pratty blue eye;
Anïŋ― I says ïŋ―I mun gie tha a kiss,ïŋ― anïŋ― Sally says ïŋ―Noïŋ―, thou moïŋ―nt,ïŋ―
But I gied ïŋ―er a kiss, anïŋ― then anoother, anïŋ― Sally says ïŋ―doïŋ―nt!ïŋ―

IX.
Anïŋ― when we coomïŋ―d into Meeïŋ―tinïŋ―, at fust she wur all in a tew,
But, arter, we singïŋ―d the ïŋ―ymn togither like birds on a beugh;
Anïŋ― Muggins ïŋ―e preïŋ―chïŋ―d oïŋ― Hell-fire anïŋ― the loov oïŋ― God fur men,
Anïŋ― then upoïŋ― coominïŋ― awaïŋ―y Sally gied me a kiss ov ïŋ―ersen.

X.
Heer wur a fall froïŋ― a kiss to a kick like Saïŋ―tan as fell
Down out oïŋ― heaven iïŋ― hell-fireïŋ―thaw theerïŋ―s naw drinkinïŋ― iïŋ― Hell;
Meïŋ― fur to kick our Sally as kep the wolf froïŋ― the door,
All along oïŋ― the drink, fur I loovïŋ―d ïŋ―er as well as afoor.

XI.
Sa like a greïŋ―t num-cumpus I blubberïŋ―d awaïŋ―y oïŋ― the bedïŋ―
ïŋ―Weïŋ―nt niver do it naw moor;ïŋ― anïŋ― Sally loookt up anïŋ― she said,
ïŋ―Iïŋ―ll upowd it8 tha weïŋ―nt; thouïŋ―rt like the rest oïŋ― the men,
Thouïŋ―ll goïŋ― sniffinïŋ― about the tap till tha does it agïŋ―an.
Theerïŋ―s thy hennemy, man, anïŋ― I knaws, as knaws tha sa well,
That, if tha seeïŋ―s ïŋ―im anïŋ― smells ïŋ―im thaïŋ―ll foller ïŋ―im slick into Hell.ïŋ―

XII.
ïŋ―Naïŋ―y,ïŋ― says I, ïŋ―fur I weïŋ―nt goïŋ― sniffinïŋ― about the tap.ïŋ―
ïŋ―Weïŋ―nt tha?ïŋ― she says, anïŋ― mysen I thowt iïŋ― mysen ïŋ―mayhap.ïŋ―
ïŋ―Noïŋ―:ïŋ― anïŋ― I started awaïŋ―y like a shot, anïŋ― down to the Hinn,
Anïŋ― I browt what tha seeïŋ―s stanninïŋ― theer, yon big black bottle oïŋ― gin.

XIII.
ïŋ―That caps owt,ïŋ―9 says Sally, anïŋ― saw she begins to cry,
But I puts it inter ïŋ―er ïŋ―ands anïŋ― I says to ïŋ―er, ïŋ―Sally,ïŋ― says I,
ïŋ―Stanïŋ― ïŋ―im theer iïŋ― the naïŋ―me oïŋ― the Lord anïŋ― the power ov ïŋ―is Graïŋ―ce,
Stanïŋ― ïŋ―im theer, fur Iïŋ―ll looïŋ―k my hennemy strait iïŋ― the faïŋ―ce,
Stanïŋ― ïŋ―im theer iïŋ― the winder, anïŋ― let ma looïŋ―k at ïŋ―im then,
ïŋ―E seeïŋ―ms naw moor nor watter, anïŋ― ïŋ―eïŋ―s the Divilïŋ―s oïŋ―n sen.ïŋ―

XIV.
Anïŋ― I wur down iïŋ― tha mouth, couldnïŋ―t do naw work anïŋ― all,
Nasty anïŋ― snaggy anïŋ― shaïŋ―ky, anïŋ― poonchïŋ―d my ïŋ―and wiïŋ― the hawl,
But she wur a power oïŋ― coomfut, anïŋ― sattled ïŋ―ersen oïŋ― my knee,
Anïŋ― coïŋ―xd anïŋ― coodled me oop till ageïŋ―n I feelïŋ―d mysen free.

XV.
Anïŋ― Sally she tellïŋ―d it about, anïŋ― foïŋ―lk stood a-gawminïŋ―10 in,
As thaw it wur summat bewitchïŋ―d istead of a quart oïŋ― gin;
Anïŋ― some on ïŋ―em said it wur watterïŋ―anïŋ― I wur chousinïŋ― the wife,
Fur I couldnïŋ―t ïŋ―owd ïŋ―ands off gin, wur it nobbut to saïŋ―ve my life;
Anïŋ― blacksmith ïŋ―e strips me the thick ov ïŋ―is airm, anïŋ― ïŋ―e shaws it to me,
Feeïŋ―l thou this! thou canïŋ―t graw this upoïŋ― watter!ïŋ― says he.
Anïŋ― Doctor ïŋ―e calls oïŋ― Sunday anïŋ― just as candles was lit,
ïŋ―Thou moïŋ―nt do it,ïŋ― he says, ïŋ―tha mun break ïŋ―im off bit by bit.ïŋ―
ïŋ―Thouïŋ―rt but a Methody-man,ïŋ― says Parson, and laïŋ―ys down ïŋ―is ïŋ―at,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―e points to the bottle oïŋ― gin, ïŋ―but I respeeks tha fur that;ïŋ―
Anïŋ― Squire, his oïŋ―n very sen, walks down froïŋ― the ïŋ―All to see,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―e spanks ïŋ―is ïŋ―and into mine, ïŋ―fur I respecks tha,ïŋ― says ïŋ―e;
Anïŋ― coostom ageïŋ―n drawïŋ―d in like a wind froïŋ― far anïŋ― wide,
And browt me the booïŋ―ts to be cobbled froïŋ― hafe the coontryside.

XVI.
Anïŋ― theer ïŋ―e stans anïŋ― theer ïŋ―e shall stan to my dying daïŋ―y;
I ïŋ―a gotten to loov ïŋ―im ageïŋ―n in anoother kind of a waïŋ―y,
Proud on ïŋ―im, like, my lad, anïŋ― I keeïŋ―ps ïŋ―im cleïŋ―n anïŋ― bright,
Loovs ïŋ―im, anïŋ― roobs ïŋ―im, anïŋ― doosts ïŋ―im, anïŋ― puts ïŋ―im back iïŋ― the light.

XVII.
Wouldnïŋ―t a pint aïŋ― sarved as well as a quart? Naw doubt:
But I liked a bigger fetter to fight wiïŋ― an fowt it out.
Fine anïŋ― meller ïŋ―e mun be by this, if I cared to taïŋ―ste,
But I moïŋ―nt, my lad, and I weïŋ―nt, fur Iïŋ―d feïŋ―l mysen cleïŋ―n disgraïŋ―ced.

XVIII.
Anïŋ― once I said to the Missis, ïŋ―My lass, when I cooms to die,
Smash the bottle to smithers, the Divilïŋ―s in ïŋ―im,ïŋ― said I.
But arter I chaïŋ―nged my mind, anïŋ― if Sally be left aloïŋ―n,
Iïŋ―ll hev ïŋ―im a-buried wiïŋ―mma anïŋ― taïŋ―kt ïŋ―im afoor the Throïŋ―n.

XIX.
Coom thou ïŋ―eerïŋ―yon laïŋ―dy a-steppin along the streeïŋ―t,
Doesnïŋ―t tha knaw ïŋ―erïŋ―sa pratty, anïŋ― feïŋ―t, anïŋ― neïŋ―t, anïŋ― sweeïŋ―t?
Look at the cloïŋ―ths on ïŋ―er back, thebbe ammost spick-span-new,
Anïŋ― Tommyïŋ―s faïŋ―ce be as fresh as a codlin weshïŋ―d iïŋ― the dew.

XX.
ïŋ―Ere he our Sally anïŋ― Tommy, anïŋ― we be a-goin to dine,
Baïŋ―con anïŋ― taïŋ―tes, anïŋ― a beslings-pud-dinïŋ―11 anïŋ― Adamïŋ―s wine;
But if tha wants ony grog tha mun goïŋ― fur it down to the Hinn,
Fur I weïŋ―nt shed a drop on ïŋ―is blood, noïŋ―, not fur Sallyïŋ―s oïŋ―n kin.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
